title: Tool Design & Custom Tool Development
learningObjectives: Design effective tool interfaces for agents
Build tools that are robust, informative on failure, and safe
Create compound tools and tool pipelines
Implement tool with authentication and rate limiting
Write tool schemas that guide correct model behavior

sections.title: Tool Design Is Agent Design
sections.content: The quality of your agent is largely determined by the quality of its tools. A poorly designed tool — vague description, unhelpful error messages, wrong return format — will cause the model to misuse it, leading to cascading failures. A well-designed tool is like a well-designed API: clear contract, predictable behavior, rich error information, and safe by default. This day focuses on the craft of tool design: what makes a tool agent-friendly, how to handle errors in a way the model can recover from, and how to build compound tools that reduce the number of calls needed.

sections.title: Principles of Agent-Friendly Tool Design
sections.content:
**The 6 rules of agent-friendly tools:**

1. **Description is everything** — the model selects tools based on description. Describe: WHEN to use it, WHAT it returns, its LIMITATIONS.
   ```
   Bad:  'Search for information'
   Good: 'Search the web for current facts, news, and public data. Returns up to 5 
          result snippets with titles and URLs. Do NOT use for internal company data.'
   ```

2. **Return structured data, not prose** — JSON with labeled fields lets the model reliably extract values. Prose requires the model to parse natural language.
   ```python
   # Bad:  return 'The weather in Paris is 15°C and cloudy'
   # Good: return json.dumps({'city': 'Paris', 'temp_c': 15, 'condition': 'cloudy'})
   ```

3. **Fail informatively** — errors must tell the model how to recover, not just that something failed.
   ```python
   # Bad:  return 'Error'
   # Good: return json.dumps({'error': 'Product not found', 'suggestion': 'Try searching by SKU instead of name'})
   ```

4. **Be idempotent where possible** — calling the tool twice with the same input should produce the same result (or harmlessly fail). Avoids double-mutations if the model retries.

5. **One tool, one responsibility** — a tool that does 5 things requires complex argument handling and confuses the model.

6. **Guard against misuse** — validate inputs, limit scope (no SQL injection, no path traversal), log every call.

**Tool schema best practices:**
```python
# Use enums to constrain choices
'status': {'type': 'string', 'enum': ['pending', 'shipped', 'delivered']}

# Use descriptions in every property
'user_id': {'type': 'string', 'description': 'UUID of the user, e.g. "user_abc123"'}

# Mark required fields explicitly
'required': ['product_id']  # don't list optional fields here

# Use number with constraints
'limit': {'type': 'integer', 'minimum': 1, 'maximum': 100, 'default': 10}
```

**Tool result format standards:**
```python
# Success: always include what was found/done
{'success': True, 'data': {...}, 'count': N}

# Error: always include error_code + human message + recovery hint
{'success': False, 'error_code': 'NOT_FOUND', 'message': 'Order ORD-123 does not exist', 'suggestion': 'Check the order ID and try again'}

# Partial success: clearly distinguish available vs missing data
{'success': True, 'data': {...}, 'warnings': ['Price data unavailable — showing last known price from 2024-01-01']}
```

sections.title: Production-Grade Tool Library

items.mistake: Generic error returns like 'Error: Something went wrong'
items.why: The model cannot recover from a vague error. It doesn't know whether to retry, try a different tool, or ask the user.
items.fix: Every error must include: error_code (machine-readable), message (human-readable), suggestion (how to recover). Example: {error_code: 'NOT_FOUND', message: 'Customer C999 does not exist', suggestion: 'Verify the customer ID. Use list_customers tool to see valid IDs.'}

items.mistake: Tools that accept free-form strings for constrained choices
items.why: If your tool accepts any string for 'status' but only processes 'pending', 'shipped', 'delivered', the model may pass 'in-transit' and get a silent failure.
items.fix: Use JSON Schema enums for constrained fields. The model will only choose valid values: `'status': {'type': 'string', 'enum': ['pending', 'shipped', 'delivered']}`

items.mistake: Side-effectful tools with no safety guards
items.why: A tool that sends emails or modifies a database can cause damage if called incorrectly (wrong recipient, wrong data).
items.fix: Implement dry_run mode. Validate all inputs before executing side effects. Log all calls. For irreversible actions, require explicit confirmation parameter: `'confirm': {'type': 'boolean', 'description': 'Must be true to execute'}'

items.mistake: Tool description says nothing about when NOT to use the tool
items.why: The model may use a tool in situations it wasn't designed for, producing wrong results or unexpected side effects.
items.fix: Add explicit 'Do NOT use for...' clauses to tool descriptions. Example: 'Do NOT use for real-time data — results may be cached up to 1 hour.'

sections.title: Interview Questions

conceptual.question: What makes a good tool description for an agent?
conceptual.answer: A good tool description answers: (1) WHAT it does — the primary action. (2) WHEN to use it — specific scenarios where it's appropriate. (3) WHEN NOT to use it — explicit anti-cases to prevent misuse. (4) WHAT it returns — the shape and meaning of the output. (5) LIMITATIONS — latency, data freshness, rate limits. Example: 'Search the product catalog by name or SKU. Returns price, stock level, and category. Use for: product lookups, inventory checks. Do NOT use for: order history or customer data — use query_database instead. Returns cached data (up to 10 min stale).'
followUps: How does the length of a tool description affect model behavior?

conceptual.question: Why should tool return values be structured JSON rather than natural language prose?
conceptual.answer: Structured JSON lets the model reliably extract specific values using field names: `result['stock']` is unambiguous. Prose requires the model to parse natural language like 'There are 150 units of Widget Pro available at $29.99' — which may be misread or requires an extra parsing step. JSON also makes it easy to programmatically validate, log, and test tool outputs. For tools in a pipeline (tool A's output feeds tool B), structured data avoids lossy text-to-text conversion.
followUps: Are there cases where natural language tool output is better?

codeBased.question: How would you implement idempotency in a 'place_order' tool?

seniorScenario.question: You're designing a suite of 20 tools for a customer support agent. How do you ensure the agent selects the right tool without getting confused?
seniorScenario.answer: Tool taxonomy and design: (1) Group tools by domain: order tools (get_order, list_orders, cancel_order), customer tools (get_customer, update_customer), product tools (search_products, check_inventory). Name them with domain prefix so the agent understands the grouping. (2) Mutual exclusion in descriptions: each tool explicitly states what it does NOT cover and what other tool to use instead. (3) A 'router' tool as first call: 'classify_query' returns the best domain + tool to use, reducing the 20-choice decision to 2 steps. (4) Reduce the tool list: combine tools that are always used together into compound tools (get_order_details = order + customer + products in one call). Goal: 20 tools → 10 focused tools + 1 router. (5) Test tool selection separately: run 100 sample queries through the agent, check which tool was selected, measure correct-tool rate. Iterate on descriptions until correct-tool rate > 95%.
followUps: How do you handle a user query that requires multiple tools from different domains?

wrongAnswers: Tools should accept natural language instructions instead of structured parameters for flexibility — structured parameters with clear schemas produce more reliable model behavior

Compound tools are always better than separate tools because they reduce API calls — compound tools increase complexity and make it harder for the model to understand what each tool does; use them only for commonly co-accessed data
Rate limiting tools is unnecessary since the model will naturally space out calls — without rate limiting, an agent processing 100 queries concurrently will overwhelm the tool's backend

questions.question: What is the most important element of a tool's schema for correct model behavior?

options.B: The description — it determines when and how the model chooses to call the tool
options.C: The return type — the model parses it differently based on type
options.D: The parameter names — longer names are better

questions.explanation: The model selects and uses tools based entirely on their descriptions. A perfect schema with a vague description produces poor tool selection. Descriptions are the primary interface between the tool and the model's decision-making.

questions.question: A tool call fails and returns 'Error: Database connection failed'. What should the agent do?

options.A: Stop the task and report failure
options.C: Use the error to decide whether to retry, try an alternative, or ask for help — but the vague error doesn't give enough information
options.D: Call a different tool that also connects to the database

questions.explanation: Vague error messages prevent recovery. The agent doesn't know if it's a transient failure (retry), a credential issue (can't retry), or a wrong input (fix the query). Well-designed tools return {error_code, message, suggestion} to enable targeted recovery.

questions.question: What is an idempotent tool and why does it matter for agents?

options.A: A tool that runs in constant time
options.B: A tool that produces the same result when called multiple times with the same input
options.C: A tool that doesn't require authentication
options.D: A tool that caches all its results

questions.explanation: Idempotent tools are safe to retry — if the agent calls place_order twice due to a network error, an idempotent implementation returns the original order rather than creating a duplicate. Critical for reliability in agentic systems where network failures and retries are common.

questions.question: Your agent keeps calling search_products when it should call get_order. Both have similar descriptions. What's the fix?

options.A: Rename the tools to avoid similar words
options.B: Add explicit 'Do NOT use for orders' to search_products and 'Do NOT use for catalog search' to get_order
options.D: Force the model to use get_order with tool_choice

questions.explanation: Explicit negative clauses ('Do NOT use for X') are the most effective way to prevent cross-tool confusion. They tell the model precisely when not to use a tool and implicitly guide it to the alternative.

questions.question: Why should a 'send_email' tool always create a draft rather than send immediately?

options.A: Draft creation is faster than sending
options.B: Email sending is an irreversible, externally-visible action that requires human review before execution
options.D: Most email providers don't support direct sending via API

questions.explanation: Sending an email is irreversible — you can't un-send it. An agent may compose an incorrect or inappropriate email. Always surface drafts for human review before sending. This is the 'minimal footprint, prefer reversible actions' safety principle.
